oral.description: The leaders of the practice are formally or informally identified.
The practice has a mission statement that reflects its strategic objectives and matches the needs of the community served.
The leaders are collectively responsible for ensuring that the mission statement is known to all personnel, patients, carers and the community served.
The practice participates in community health activities.
The leaders, in liaison with the whole practice team, coordinates the compilation of an annual strategic plan and budget.
The leaders are collectively responsible for implementing the practice's mission and strategic plan.
The strategic plan is evaluated on an annual basis.
Regular monitoring of the implementation of the strategic plan against envisaged timeframes and progress in achieving its objectives is documented at intervals determined by the practice.
Where appropriate, the leadership roles in various positions are documented, agreed to and known by the personnel.
The manager is responsible for the day-to-day running of the practice.
The manager has the education and/or experience necessary to carry out his or her responsibilities.
The practice is licensed in terms of relevant legislation for the level and type of services provided.
The manager ensures that there is a system in place to monitor the implementation of applicable laws and regulations.
There is evidence of response to any reports from inspecting and regulatory authorities.
The manager implements processes to manage and control human, financial and other resources.
The practice leaders facilitate communication between teams where relevant and between individual personnel members.
Agendas for meetings are prepared and shared in order to allow those attending to prepare for participation.
Minutes of meetings are taken and circulated to all relevant personnel.
There is a procedure to make sure that important matters resulting from management meetings are communicated to and acted upon by personnel.

The practice plans and coordinates comprehensive care by establishing relations and maintaining contact with other relevant services and agencies, including both governmental and nongovernmental agencies.
Information on services, hours of operation and processes for obtaining care is provided to services in the community who work in collaboration with the practice.
The practice determines that the receiving individual/organisation can meet the patient's continuing care needs and establishes arrangements to ensure continuity.
Policies and procedures that guide and support the different services offered by the practice are implemented.
Policies and procedures are signed and dated by persons authorised to do so.
Policies and procedures are correctly compiled, indexed and filed.
Policies and procedures are reviewed according to practice policy and then dated and signed.
There is a process to ensure that personnel are familiar with relevant policies and procedures.
There are documented processes for staffing the practice.
The desired education, qualifications, skills and knowledge are defined for personnel.
The processes include measures to improve personnel retention.
There is implementation of a reliable, documented process for evaluating and verifying the credentials of all health professionals.
Personnel files contain copies of qualifications and licences/registration from the relevant authority for all health professionals.
There is a system to track the annual registration of all health professionals.
All personnel with direct contact with the public have had a police check, a copy of which is kept in their personnel file.
There are documented processes for orientation of personnel to the practice.
The practice supports continuing education for its clinical personnel and maintains records of this in personnel files.
There is a development strategy for the practice that ensures that the practice manager and administrative personnel receive the training required to fulfil their responsibilities.
Personnel members are informed of opportunities to participate in advanced education, training, research, and other experiences.
A designated person is responsible for the implementation and maintenance of a financial strategy.
The designated is suitably qualified and/or experienced in accounting and financial management.
The responsibilities of this person include ensuring that policies and procedures for all financial functions are implemented.
There is a current budget for the practice.
A report is produced at least annually, setting out the financial position to date.
There is a mechanism for establishing the reason for budget variation in either income or expenditure.
Capital investment proposals are subject to unanimous agreement among the partners or are agreed according to a voting system acceptable to all partners in the practice.
The practice has documented ethical and legal policies and procedures for the financial management of the practice that are implemented.
Internal and external financial audit systems which meet audit requirements are maintained.
Annual audited financial statements are produced within the required time frame.
There is a capital asset register, which is routinely maintained.
Assets are insured.
All health professionals provide evidence of professional indemnity insurance.
An individual is designated to control the ordering, storage, distribution and control of equipment and supplies used in the practice.
Policies and procedures relating to all aspects of provisioning/supply chain management are implemented.
Secure storage facilities are available.
Prescription pads, letterheads, investigation request forms, administrative records and other official documents are accessible only to authorised persons.
There are documented risk management processes for identifying all risks relating to practice processes and systems, personnel, patients, visitors to the practice and physical facilities.
The practice manager ensures the development and implementation of written policies and procedures for risk management processes and activities.
A nominated individual with relevant qualifications, skills and/or experience supervises the implementation of the risk management programme.
Ongoing in-service training of all personnel in risk management policies, procedures and principles is documented.
Risk management systems are reviewed whenever there are changes in practice systems and processes or physical facilities.
A nominated individual is responsible for infection control in the practice and practice personnel are aware of the nomination.
Written policies and procedures guide personnel in the implementation of the infection control programme.
Regular in-service training is given to all personnel in the field of infection control and is documented.
Infection control is on the agenda of all personnel meetings of the practice and discussion points are documented.
All patient and personnel areas of the practice are included in the documented infection control programme.
Hand washing and disinfecting facilities, including warm water, soap, paper towels or hand sanitisers are available in all relevant areas.
Personnel are constantly reminded of the importance of effective hand washing.
A nominated individual has been trained in and is responsible for sterilisation procedures within the practice and can describe the process in detail.
Relevant personnel members are immunised against Hepatitis B according to the policy.
There is a documented policy for the management of exposure to high-risk infections and needle stick injuries.
Post exposure prophylaxis is available to personnel in accordance with national policy and includes the management of the patient from whom the needle was withdrawn.
There is a documented policy for the management of body fluid spills.
There is a documented policy for the triage of patients with potential communicable diseases.
The practice has a waste management policy that is consistent with country-specific laws and regulations and includes the safe handling, storing and disposing of all different types of waste.
The policy makes provision for the appropriate management of confidential waste.
Waste is segregated in accordance with policies, procedures, country-specific laws and regulations.
The colour of bag and type of container appropriate to the type of waste generated are available.
Waste is protected from theft, vandalism or scavenging by animals.
Waste is collected at appropriate times so that hazards are not caused.
Security systems, including guards if required, provide for internal and external security.
Sufficient light sources are available to provide adequate light (no dark areas) in all areas such as the entrance, waiting rooms, halls and offices.
There is effective control of access to clinical areas and store areas.
Alarm systems and signals are tested every month.
A mechanism known to the personnel is available for summoning the assistance of security/police/protection services in the case of an emergency.
Reasonable measures are taken to ensure the safety of lone workers.
There are structured systems and processes in place to ensure that all occupants of the practice's facilities are safe from fire or smoke.
Documented certification is available from the relevant authority to show that the facility complies with applicable laws and regulations in relation to fire safety (fire clearance certificate).
Firefighting equipment is regularly inspected and serviced at least annually, and the date of the service is recorded on the apparatus.
Flammable materials are clearly labelled and safely stored.
Sufficient electrical socket outlets are provided in all areas to avoid overloading of individual outlets and to minimise fire risks.
Easily recognised and understood signs prohibiting smoking are displayed in areas where flammable materials and combustible gases are stored.
A floor plan showing the location of firefighting equipment, electrical distribution board, evacuation routes and emergency exits is displayed.
Annual personnel training in fire prevention and evacuation procedures is documented.
A mechanism known to personnel is available for summoning the fire service.
There is a written plan to deal with emergencies (including bomb threats, flooding, natural disasters, failure of water and electrical supplies).
There are site and floor plans that depict the locations and layout of the main services (for example, water, sanitation, electricity supply).
Documented evidence is available to show that the personnel participate in a rehearsal of the emergency plan at least annually.
First aid kits and materials for healthcare workers are available.
There is a documented policy for collection, collation, validation and distribution of data which is implemented.
Clinical, managerial and administrative personnel participate in developing and implementing an information system to support patient care and practice management.
Clinical and managerial data and information are integrated as needed to support decision-making.
Required technology and other resources support the implementation of the data and information management system.
Security and confidentiality of data and information is maintained.
Documented procedures which outline the processes to provide required information to individuals and agencies outside the practice when required by laws or regulations are implemented.
The practice manager or delegated person checks the integrity of data leaving the facility for completeness, correctness and consistency.
Data is aggregated, analysed and transformed into useful, relevant information for monitoring and improving the service.
The frequency of data collection and analysis is appropriate to the process under study.
Statistical tools and techniques are used in the analysis process when suitable.
Information relating to the quality of the services delivered by the practice is made available to the patients of the practice and other relevant parties.
A designated individual oversees the information and quality management and improvement processes.
All practice personnel are informed about the function of the information and quality management individual.
There are formal systems and processes for quality management and improvement.
Personnel are trained in the implementation of quality management processes.

The practice manager provides technology, support and resources (including time) for the assessment and improvement of the practice’s management, clinical and support processes and this is reflected in the strategic plan/business plan for the practice.
The leaders set priorities for improvement activities based on high risk, high cost and/or high volume or problem-prone areas.
Each function within the practice implements relevant quality improvement activities.
The objectives, scope, implementation and effectiveness of the activities to assess and improve quality are evaluated regularly and revised as necessary.
Targets (goals) are set for the desired levels of patient care and practice management.
The practice collects data relevant to each identified indicator in the quality improvement programme for the monitoring and improvement of patient care and practice management.
As part of clinical monitoring, structured clinical audits are done to monitor the implementation of clinical guidelines.
Information from the findings of quality assessment and improvement activities is used to detect trends, patterns and opportunities to improve or prevent potential problems.
When appropriate, an improvement plan is developed in collaboration with all relevant team members and an implementation process and acceptable timeframe is agreed to by the team and implemented.
A time for repeat data collection and analysis is agreed and completed and the results discussed by the relevant team members.
The practice holds regular meetings to discuss significant clinical issues.
Information from a validated patient/family satisfaction audit tool is used to improve the quality of service delivery.

A documented procedure for the monitoring of near misses/incidents/adverse (sentinel) events is available, which includes the documentation of interventions, responses and corrective actions taken to recorded incidents.
Formal significant event analyses are undertaken when necessary.
Notes are kept regarding the data analysis and actions arising from formal incident reviews.
All changes to policy and procedure that are identified as a result of these formal incident reviews are documented and included in new policies or incorporated into existing policies.
The implementation of these new policies/procedures is delegated to a nominated individual who is responsible for monitoring the effectiveness of the changes and arranging reviews if appropriate.
Patient records are reviewed regularly, and results analysed as part of the quality improvement process.
The review uses a representative sample.
Records comply with professionally acceptable norms (including legal requirements where applicable) relating to signature, use of abbreviations and legibility.
Standardised diagnosis and procedure codes are used.
Symbols and definitions are standardised.
Directional signs to the practice are clearly readable and up to date.
A telephone/emergency number is available and provided to patients on registration and on request.
A phone number for the after-hours service of the practice is clearly displayed on the outside of the facility.
Parking is provided close to the building entrance for patients, including the physically challenged.
There is wheelchair access to and within the building.
Ramps and stairs include safety features such as rails.
Laws, regulations and other requirements applicable to the practice's facilities are implemented and are available to the personnel.
The building is appropriate as a healthcare facility in terms of size and lay-out.
The waiting area is sufficient to accommodate the usual number of patients and other people who could be waiting at any given time.
The waiting area caters for the specific safety needs of children.
There is at least one treatment room for every member of the clinical team working in the practice at any time.
All areas of the facility, including treatment rooms are clean, well ventilated and well maintained.
Sufficient office/administrative space is available for the personnel.
Toilet/washroom facilities are clean and in working order.
Separate sanitary facilities are provided for personnel.
There is a separate, secure area for personnel with adequate secure storage facilities for outdoor clothing, handbags and personal possessions.
Required furniture is available according to established lists and functioning properly.
Hand washing facilities, including warm water, soap and paper towels are available for patients and personnel.
Implemented policies and procedures guide the management of medical equipment.
A designated individual supervises the management of medical equipment in the practice.
There is an inventory of the medical equipment available at the practice.
The supply of medical equipment is adequate to meet the needs of the practice.
Records are kept of the checking and maintenance of medical equipment.
There is a documented procedure known to the personnel for reporting defects in medical equipment.
Written policies and procedures guide practice personnel on the implementation of all maintenance service requirements.
A designated, competent individual is responsible for supervising the maintenance of buildings, grounds and utilities.
Where these services are outsourced, the practice personnel have access to a list of these private contractors/service providers with their contact numbers at all times.
Written agreements ensure technical back-up services are available at all times during the opening or operational hours of the practice.
Basic maintenance equipment, tools and spare parts are available.
The practice plans and budgets for the upgrading or replacing of systems, buildings or components needed for the continued operation of a safe and effective facility.
The practice has a documented preventative maintenance management plan in place.
Documented inspections of the buildings and grounds are conducted at regular intervals as determined by the practice policy.
There is a documented procedure known to the personnel for reporting defects.
Policies and procedures that guide the management of ICT equipment are implemented.
A designated individual supervises the management of ICT equipment in the practice.
There is an inventory of all ICT equipment.
All desktop and server computers are provided with surge protection and the server is protected by an uninterruptable power supply.
A documented policy is available clearly describing appropriate back up procedures for electronic records.
Regular checks are made and documented to ensure that backup has been successful.
Records are kept of the checking and maintenance of ICT equipment.
The practice has appropriate and up to date virus protection software and firewall protection to ensure adequate security and confidentiality of patient related information.
There is documented evidence that relevant personnel are regularly trained to use/operate ICT equipment.
Policies and procedures for the storage, retrieval, retention, destruction and confidentiality and safety of patient records and personal information are implemented.
Designated individuals are responsible for the storage, maintenance and retrieval of patient files.
Storage space for health records is sufficient and secure against unauthorised entry.
Records that have been removed from the designated storage area for use are kept out of public view.
The retention process provides the necessary confidentiality and security.
Written policies and procedures relating to cleaning duties and the frequency with which these duties are carried out are implemented and monitored.
Adequate, secure and well-ventilated storage areas are available for cleaning and equipment and chemicals.
Mops and brooms are cleaned and dried before being stored.
The practice manager ensures that cleaning personnel are appropriately trained regarding waste management, infection control procedures, confidentiality issues and any other relevant matters.
Copies of contracts for outside service providers are available to those who ensure they are implemented.
Services provided under contracts and other arrangements are formally monitored and compliance with the contract is documented.
Contracted personnel are managed as determined in the written service agreement.
The practice ensures that contracted personnel are oriented to the practice and to relevant practice policies and procedures.
The practice ensures that contracted personnel participate in relevant practice in-service training programmes.
The clinical team uses current clinical guidelines relevant to its practice, to assist in the diagnosis and management of patients.
The clinical team set criteria to select clinical practice guidelines.
The clinical team can describe how they ensure consistency of diagnosis and management of patients.
The clinical team can demonstrate how they communicate about clinical issues and support systems in the practice.
When patients register with the practice they are informed of the range of services offered by the practice, opening hours, contact details and after hours’ care arrangements.
Where the practice has an 'on hold' telephone message, it includes a message for an alternative number to be used in an emergency.
A message on the practice's telephone answering machine, call diversion system or paging system provide information to patients on how to obtain care outside the practice's normal opening hours.
The practice renders services based on the needs of the population, during the hours published.
There is a documented process to refer patients.
The lines of communication between the practice, referral facility and community services are clearly defined.
The clinician informs the patient if he/she has any financial interest in the referral service.
Referrals are to specific individuals and/or agencies in the patient's home community wherever possible.
Patients and as appropriate their families are given follow-up instructions which are provided in an understandable form and manner.
A copy of the referral note is available in the patient record.
The referral is made on appropriate stationery or electronically.
The referral contains as a minimum a) – g) in the standard intent above.
The practice keeps a record of replies received following the referral of patients
There are written guidelines for the referral of emergency patients.
Follow-up care based on the findings of investigations/consultations performed outside the practice are noted in the patient record.
There is a flexible appointment system for consultations.
There is a documented system for fast-tracking the very ill, the elderly, the frail and pregnant women.
Patients who are waiting are advised of any delays that may be experienced in receiving attention.
There is a system to ensure that patients are seen within the shortest possible time.
Patients are offered longer appointments when clinically indicated.
The patient is offered an appointment with their preferred clinician when possible.
The patient's consent is obtained if third parties are expected to be present during the consultation.
New patients to the practice are asked to complete a form detailing at least a) – o) in the standard intent above.
Patients are asked to update the practice if their contact details change.
Health funder details are accurately recorded for each new patient when required.
The practice implements a policy on telephonic and electronic communication with patients that details at least a) – e) in the standard intent above.
There is evidence of practice/patient telephone or electronic advice and information in the patient health records.
The practice can demonstrate how it receives and returns telephone and electronic messages from patients.
A documented policy on the review and management of pathology results, imaging reports, investigation reports and other clinical correspondence received by the practice is implemented.

Patient health records contain evidence that all pathology results, imaging reports, investigation reports and clinical correspondence received by the practice have been handled in accordance with practice policy.
There is evidence of review of the results of procedures and diagnostic tests performed by a Dental practitioner.
There is a system to advise patients of the process to follow up on results.
A documented system to identify, follow up and recall patients with clinically significant results is implemented.
When patients are informed of results over the telephone, the patient is identified with 3 approved identifiers before the information is given.
When results are given over the telephone, the result is given to the patient themselves or the patient’s consent is obtained before giving the result to a nominated third party.
The practice offers relevant health education to its patients in a planned and consistent manner which enables patients to meet their ongoing health needs and achieve their health goals.

Posters and pamphlets regarding high risk and commonly treated conditions relevant to the practice population are visibly displayed and accessible in the waiting room and routinely provided to patients during the consultation when appropriate.
Patient and family education provided is noted in the patient record.
Information is provided in a manner and in a language that is understood by those making the care decisions.
Notes for each consultation contain as a minimum points a) – o) above.
Handwritten notes are legible.
Notes are recorded contemporaneously or instantly.
All abbreviations are standardised in accordance with recognised local and national standards.
The patients' records, including contact details, are up to date to ensure the transfer of the latest information between care providers
Adverse drug reactions are noted in the patient's record.
The patient is fully informed regarding the estimated costs of any treatment, investigation, procedure or referral and the discussion is documented.
There is implementation of a documented policy on clinical handover to ensure that standard processes are followed.
Patients share in decision making regarding the handing over of clinical information.
Handover of clinical information is recorded in the patients' health records.
There is evidence of one (or a combination) of arrangements for patients to access care outside of normal opening hours.
The credentials of alternative care providers are verified.
Patient health records contain reports or notes of consultations occurring outside normal opening hours by or on behalf of the practice.
Written guidelines are available and followed relating to the provision of primary emergency services.
Guidelines for emergency triage, assessment and treatment are available and followed for all types of patients seen at the practice (for example adult, paediatric and neonatal).
Information on cases and the outcome of emergency treatment are recorded in a register/logbook.
Case reviews are undertaken within the practice to assess the quality of treatment and care of patients requiring emergency care.
There is a protocol that delineates how the practice evaluates, manages, stabilises and transfers patients with emergency conditions.
The practice has an implemented resuscitation policy.
All practice personnel are trained in basic resuscitation techniques at least every two years, with records of their attendance at such training.

A designated person documents daily checks to ensure that resuscitation equipment is available, in working order and that consumables are not expired and reports any adverse findings for immediate remediation.
There is a designated resuscitation area.
There is a mechanism for the summoning of medical help in an emergency.
The practice provides resuscitation equipment according to the services provided.

Equipment for early cardiopulmonary resuscitation is available within one minute in each area of the practice and access to a defibrillator or automated external defibrillator (AED) within three minutes of any patient collapsing.
The practice has access to Ambulance Services (EMS).
A designated individual who is suitably qualified has clearly defined responsibilities and accountability for all aspects of medication management.
Medication is prescribed and in accordance with legislation and current pharmaceutical, medical and dental guidelines.
The scope of and limitations to the responsibilities and activities of the personnel who manage medication are clearly defined in written policies.
Medication is securely and legibly labelled with relevant information as required by practice policy.
The person prescribing the medication has access to patient information that would contra-indicate prescription of particular medication.
Controlled medication is prescribed in accordance with legislation and practice policy.
Verbal/telephonic medication orders are documented in the patient's record.
Prescriptions conform to all legal requirements.

Policies and procedures are implemented to ensure that medication is transported to the practice and stored within the practice according to manufacturers' guidelines, with specific emphasis on maintenance of cold chain requirements.
A dedicated refrigerator is available for medication requiring storage at low temperatures.
A monitoring log is kept of the temperature within the refrigerator and/or cold-chain monitors and is available for inspection.
The fridge thermometer is calibrated at regular intervals determined by the practice and the calibration is documented.
Any remedial action taken is recorded.
The integrity of door seals, door and hinges is checked at regular intervals and documented accordingly.
Medication storage areas are protected from heat and light and effectively ventilated.
Stored medication is legibly marked and securely labelled.
All pharmaceuticals and medical consumables are regularly checked for expiry dates and checks are recorded.
An inventory management system either manual (stock cards) or automated, is in place to monitor maximum and minimum stock levels and control stock losses.
During all phases of care, there are qualified and experienced individuals with airway management certification responsible for the patient's care.

Procedure room rosters ensure that sufficient numbers of personnel with suitable qualifications and experience are present at all times for procedure room duties, sedation assistance and recovery area duties.

Sedation is administered only by sedation practitioners who have the necessary qualifications and who have received specific theoretical and supervised practical training, including updated airway certification.
Assistance for the sedation practitioner is provided in accordance with professional guidelines.
There is direct access to the procedure room from the receiving, scrubbing-up and recovery areas.
The design of the procedure area provides space for the reception, induction of sedation, surgery, recovery (where applicable) and monitoring of patients.
The procedure area is equipped in accordance with approved requirement lists.
Access to the procedure room is controlled.
There is a system for controlling the environmental temperature that ensures safe limits for sedated patients in accordance with professional society guidelines.
There is safe and adequate storage space for pharmaceutical and surgical supplies.
There is a dedicated medication refrigerator in the procedure area, the temperature of which is measured and recorded daily.
Appropriate action is taken and recorded when the temperature of the refrigerator is outside the recommended range.
A designated individual is responsible for the sterilising and disinfecting of required items.
Autoclaves/sterilising units are appropriate to their use and comply with licensing requirements.
There is a mechanism to ensure that the required level of sterility is obtained from the methods used.
Sterilising equipment is used and maintained according to manufacturer's recommendations.
There is a system for the ordering, storage, maintenance and distribution of sterile supplies not processed on site.
There is a mechanism to ensure that sterile supplies are dated and used on a first expired first out basis.
The maintenance of sterility is checked before any sterile items are issued.
The recommendations of current guidelines guide the provision and use of sedation equipment, supplies and medication.
The recommendations of current guidelines guide the provision and use of devices to administer oxygen and assist with ventilation, where indicated.
The recommendations of current guidelines guide the provision and use of airway devices and equipment.
The recommendations of current guidelines guide the provision, maintenance and use of monitoring equipment.
The recommendations of current guidelines guide the provision and use of equipment with which to gain intravenous access and administration/infusion of sedative/analgesic medication.
Medication is available and used according to the requirements of the level of consciousness necessary to provide safe procedural sedation and analgesia.
The medication storage area is in close proximity to the procedure room.
A medication trolley with a sharps bin is available for the exclusive use of the sedation practitioner in each procedure room.
Equipment and instrumentation for surgery must be available for the level of care provided.
There is appropriate shielding and protective clothing in the presence of biohazards (including lasers) or radiographic equipment.
Hazard or warning notices are displayed.
There are recovery facilities within the procedural area.
There is at least one recovery bed/recliner chair/trolley for each procedure room.
Non-invasive vital signs monitoring is available for each bed/recliner chair/trolley of recovery area equipment complies with current guidelines.
Oxygen and vacuum facilities are available and easily accessible.
There are two electrical sockets for each bed/recliner chair/trolley.
The provision, use and maintenance of recovery area equipment complies with current guidelines.
Clinical practice guidelines, relevant to the patients and services of the procedure room are available to guide patient care processes.
The implementation of guidelines is monitored as part of a structured clinical audit.
Guidelines are reviewed and adapted on a regular basis according to practice policy.
There is a documented process for obtaining informed consent which is implemented.
A legally valid, informed consent document for surgical or other invasive procedures is completed comprehensively and available in the patient records.
A legally valid, informed consent document is obtained by the sedation practitioner before any sedative medication is administered.
There is a pre-sedation assessment and examination, including evaluation of a medical history questionnaire, to evaluate risk and indication for sedation need for the specific patient.
The medical history questionnaire and preoperative patient assessment are used to assist the sedation practitioner to identify the possible risks of allergic or adverse medication interactions.
The patient/guardian signs for receipt of documented pre- and post-procedure information and instructions.
A qualified sedation practitioner and observer, where indicated, monitor the patient during sedation and during recovery from sedation and documents all parameters monitored.
The patient's haemodynamic status is continuously monitored and recorded during sedation, surgery and recovery.
The sedation medication used is entered on to the patient’s sedation flow-sheet.
Analgesia administered on conclusion of the surgical procedure and during recovery, is documented in the patient's record.
The sedation practitioner is responsible for supervising the recovery period and for discharging the patient according to established criteria.
Monitoring is appropriate to assess the patient's condition during the post-sedation recovery period.
Monitoring findings are documented in the patient's record.
The sedation practitioner gives permission for the patient to be discharged.
Times of arrival in and discharge from the recovery area are recorded.
Details of continued care are provided in writing to the person accompanying the patient at discharge.
The patient is taken from the procedure room to their transport by wheelchair.
Patient and family rights are identified in a policy and documented in accordance with relevant and current laws, charters and regulations.
Documented evidence of personnel training in relation to these policies and procedures is available.
There is a patient rights charter which is prominently displayed, and patients are made aware of their rights.
Patients are informed about their condition and the proposed treatment.
The practice informs patients and their families about their rights to refuse or discontinue treatment, and the consequences of such decisions.

When patients/carers refuse or discontinue treatment, the consultation regarding their decision is accurately and contemporaneously recorded and includes the details of the discussion regarding the consequences of the decision.
The patient's need for privacy is protected during all examinations, procedures and treatments.
Patient privacy is protected when providing personal information.
The patient's right to privacy is protected for all forms of counselling.
Policies and procedures to prevent the loss or misuse of patient information are implemented.
When appropriate, patients are permitted to be accompanied by a family member/care-giver during consultations.
The practice has a documented policy outlining the procedure for obtaining general consent for treatment.

The practice has a documented policy outlining the procedure for obtaining consent in the case of patients who are unable to grant consent for themselves by way of age or mental/physical incapacity, which is in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations.
Documented consent is obtained from patients for health information to be provided to a third party.
Where practice members use patient information for research, approval has been obtained from the relevant ethics committee and from patients themselves where required.
There is a documented policy outlining the mechanism to allow for the hearing of complaints and how to act upon them which is implemented.
Patients are aware of their right to voice complaints and the processes by which to do so, internally as well as externally where applicable.
A nominated individual within the practice is responsible for managing the complaints and ensuring that the complaints policy is implemented.
A nominated individual within the practice is responsible to oversee the investigation of and response to the complaint.
Complaints are monitored, and repetitions or patterns are identified.
Any opportunities for improvement identified from the investigation of complaints are implemented.
